revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Draft:Tokoyo and Kakuriyo]] to [[Draft:Tokoyo (eternal night)]]
revision.text: https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/常世

Tokoyo is also known as Kakuriyo (the world of the dead), and it is the holy precincts which is not changed forever. (It is a world without any changes, especially a steady world without the law of cause and effect or a world which can be said to have no time base in a certain part.) It means the world after the death or 'eternity,' and it was also written in Chinese characters as '常夜' (eternal night) in ancient times. The world is an important side of an antinomy in the Japanese Mythology, the Ancient Shinto, and Shinto religion, while the opposite is 'utsushiyo' (land of the living).
Summary Tokoyo' is a world which is always in the dead of the night, and it is sometimes identified with the world of the dead and yominokuni (realm of the dead) from the meaning of the Chinese characters of tokoyo (常夜). However, after the release of Shinobu ORIKUCHI's thesis, "Haha ga kuni he/ Tokoyo he" (published in 1920), it does not a mere world of the dead, but a Utopia which is considered to exist over the sea or in the sea when you mentioned especially 'Tokoyo.' It was defined as "a strange land" where you are given a fortune, knowledge, a life, a long life, and eternal youth and immortality by a visit of marebito (a god which gives people his blessing and leaves).
In the ancient Shinto, it refers to the holy precincts or a world which is different from the real world and located beyond the mountains, the seas, woods, rivers, huge trees, or megalith such as himorogi (a temporarily erected sacred space or "altar" used as a locus of worship), iwakura (dwelling place of a god, usually in reference to a large rock), and so on where 'the aspect of the place' changes.
The Japanese mythology According to "Kojiki" (The Records of Ancient Matters) and the sixth alternate writing of "Nihonshoki," Sukunabikona, who built up a country along with Okuninushi no mikoto (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region), went to tokoyo, which was across the seas, after he finished creating the country. It has changed its meaning, and now it is said that tokoyo no kuni means the land of the dead or yomi (world after death).
In "Manyoshu" (Collection of Ten Thousand Leaves), the Palace of the Dragon King where Urashimataro visited was written as tokoyo, where time passed very differently from the real world. From this, it is said to mean a paradise of eternal youth and immortality.
According to an oracle told from Amaterasu Omikami (the Sun Goddess) to Yamatohime no mikoto in "Nihonshoki," Ise is a province where the wave of tokoyo comes in.
The ancient Shinto/the bounds of a sacred place, and kinsokuchi (tabooed land) A megalith/spiritual rock, shinboku (sacred tree), Chinju-no-Mori (Sacred Shrine Forest), etc. which were considered yorishiro (spiritualistic mediums) in the ancient Shinto, indicate 'border' respectively because '籬' in himorogi (神籬) means 'a fence' and iwakura (磐座) is also written as '磐境' (rock border).
The place beyond the border is considered a sacred area, or tokoyo, and Okinoshima Island is considered sacred as a whole, including Chinju-no-Mori and kinsokuchi. Hedges of evergreen broad-leaved trees in Chinju-no-Mori and shrines are the bounds of a sacred place as well as the border of tokoyo. There are many forbidden places because people are trying not to invite a trouble or disaster caused by various things easily coming and going between utsushiyo and tokoyo.
Doso-shin (traveler's guardian deity) stone statue, small shrines, and jizo (the Guardian Deity of Travelers and Children) located at a roadside leading to a village means not only invocation of a deity for safety travel but also a barrier against tokoyo.
The meaning of tokoyo (eternal night) was assumed to be associated with a border between day and night like evening, and it was called 'Omagadoki' (twilight hour). Midnight, especially a deeply silent night, was considered to be overlapping tokoyo, so the time when something mysterious that did not exist in reality appeared was called 'ushimitsudoki' (the third quarter of the hour of the Ox (approximately 3:00 to 3:30 a.m.)), and people were afraid of the time.
The Ryukyu mythology The Ryukyu mythology has Niraikanai (paradise across the ocean [folk belief of Okinawa & Amami]) as a similar idea of different world.
Others One of the Japanese family names. Nagatane TOKOYO, etc.

== The Japanese mythology == According to "[[Kojiki]]" (The Records of Ancient Matters) and the sixth alternate writing of "[[Nihonshoki]]," [[Sukunabikona]], who built up a country along with [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi no mikoto]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region), went to tokoyo, which was across the seas, after he finished creating the country. It has changed its meaning, and now it is said that tokoyo no kuni means the land of the dead or yomi (world after death).
In "[[Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]]" (Collection of Ten Thousand Leaves), the Palace of the Dragon King where Urashimataro visited was written as tokoyo, where time passed very differently from the real world. From this, it is said to mean a paradise of eternal youth and immortality.
According to an oracle told from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]] (the Sun Goddess) to Yamatohime no mikoto in "[[Nihonshoki]]," Ise is a province where the wave of tokoyo comes in.
== The ancient Shinto/the bounds of a sacred place, and kinsokuchi (tabooed land) == A megalith/spiritual rock, [[shinboku]] (sacred tree), [[Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-Mori]] (Sacred Shrine Forest), etc. which were considered [[yorishiro]] (spiritualistic mediums) in the ancient Shinto, indicate 'border' respectively because '籬' in himorogi (神籬) means 'a fence' and iwakura (磐座) is also written as '磐境' (rock border).
The place beyond the border is considered a sacred area, or tokoyo, and Okinoshima Island is considered sacred as a whole, including Chinju-no-Mori and kinsokuchi. Hedges of evergreen broad-leaved trees in Chinju-no-Mori and shrines are the bounds of a sacred place as well as the border of tokoyo. There are many forbidden places because people are trying not to invite a trouble or disaster caused by various things easily coming and going between utsushiyo and tokoyo.
Doso-shin (traveler's guardian deity) stone statue, small shrines, and jizo (the Guardian Deity of Travelers and Children) located at a roadside leading to a village means not only invocation of a deity for safety travel but also a barrier against tokoyo.
The meaning of tokoyo (eternal night) was assumed to be associated with a border between day and night like evening, and it was called 'Omagadoki' (twilight hour). Midnight, especially a deeply silent night, was considered to be overlapping tokoyo, so the time when something mysterious that did not exist in reality appeared was called 'ushimitsudoki' (the third quarter of the hour of the Ox (approximately 3:00 to 3:30 a.m.)), and people were afraid of the time.
== Ryukyu mythology == The Ryukyu mythology has Niraikanai (paradise across the ocean [folk belief of Okinawa & Amami]) as a similar idea of different world. == 和歌 == [[Murasaki Shikibu|紫式部]] 著『[[The Tale of Genji|源氏物語]]』
: 第12帖「須磨」 :* 心から常世を捨てて鳴く雁を雲のよそにも思ひけるかな :* 常世出でて旅の空なるかりがねも列に遅れぬほどぞなぐさむ :* あかりなくにかりの常世を立ち別れ花の都に道やまどはむ : 第41帖「幻」 :* なくなくも帰りにしかな仮の世はいづこもつひの常世ならぬに
== その他 ==
* 日本の地名 ** [[Fukushima Prefecture|福島県]][[Kitakata, Fukushima|喜多方市]]塩川町常世 ** 福島県[[Higashishirakawa District, Fukushima|東白川郡]][[Hanawa, Fukushima|塙町]]常世北野 ** 福島県東白川郡塙町常世中野 * 日本人の姓氏・苗字のひとつ。常世氏。[[China (region)|中国]]系渡来人常世[[Muraji|連]]（赤染氏）の子孫、江戸時代には[[Aizu Domain|会津藩士]]にあった。常世長胤など。 * 常世岐姫神社 - 常世岐姫神を祀る神社。 * 中国古代国家"[[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|燕国]]”の別称。

(It is a world without any changes, especially a steady world without the law of cause and effect or a world which can be said to have no time base in a certain part.) {{nihongo|{{ill|Tokoyo (eternal night)|lt=Tokoyo|ja|常世}}|常世}}, also known as {{nihongo|Kakuriyo|隠世、幽世}} (the world of the dead), is a world in [[shinto]] which never changes. (It is a world without any changes, especially a steady world without the law of cause and effect or a world which can be said to have no time base in a certain part.) It means the world after the death or 'eternity,' and it was also written in Chinese characters as '常夜' (eternal night) in ancient times. The world is an important side of an [[antinomy]] in the [[Japanese Mythology]], the Ancient [[Shinto]], and Shinto religion, while the opposite is '[[Draft:This world|utsushiyo]]' (land of the living).
== Overview == Tokoyo' is a world which is always in the dead of the night, and it is sometimes identified with the world of the dead and yominokuni (realm of the dead) from the meaning of the Chinese characters of tokoyo (常夜). However, after the release of Shinobu ORIKUCHI's thesis, "Haha ga kuni he/ Tokoyo he" (published in 1920), it does not a mere world of the dead, but a Utopia which is considered to exist over the sea or in the sea when you mentioned especially 'Tokoyo.' It was defined as "a strange land" where you are given a fortune, knowledge, a life, a long life, and eternal youth and immortality by a visit of marebito (a god which gives people his blessing and leaves).
In the ancient Shinto, it refers to the holy precincts or a world which is different from the real world and located beyond the mountains, the seas, woods, rivers, huge trees, or megalith such as [[himorogi]] (a temporarily erected sacred space or "altar" used as a locus of worship), [[Iwakura rock|iwakura]] (dwelling place of a god, usually in reference to a large rock), and so on where 'the aspect of the place' changes.
== The Japanese mythology == According to "[[Kojiki]]" (The Records of Ancient Matters) and the sixth alternate writing of "[[Nihonshoki]]," [[Sukunabikona]], who built up a country along with [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi no mikoto]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region), went to tokoyo, which was across the seas, after he finished creating the country. It has changed its meaning, and now it is said that tokoyo no kuni means the land of the dead or yomi (world after death).
In "[[Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]]" (Collection of Ten Thousand Leaves), the Palace of the Dragon King where Urashimataro visited was written as tokoyo, where time passed very differently from the real world. From this, it is said to mean a paradise of eternal youth and immortality.
According to an oracle told from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]] (the Sun Goddess) to Yamatohime no mikoto in "[[Nihonshoki]]," Ise is a province where the wave of tokoyo comes in.
== The ancient Shinto/the bounds of a sacred place, and kinsokuchi (tabooed land) == A megalith/spiritual rock, [[shinboku]] (sacred tree), [[Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-Mori]] (Sacred Shrine Forest), etc. which were considered [[yorishiro]] (spiritualistic mediums) in the ancient Shinto, indicate 'border' respectively because '籬' in himorogi (神籬) means 'a fence' and iwakura (磐座) is also written as '磐境' (rock border).
The place beyond the border is considered a sacred area, or tokoyo, and Okinoshima Island is considered sacred as a whole, including Chinju-no-Mori and kinsokuchi. Hedges of evergreen broad-leaved trees in Chinju-no-Mori and shrines are the bounds of a sacred place as well as the border of tokoyo. There are many forbidden places because people are trying not to invite a trouble or disaster caused by various things easily coming and going between utsushiyo and tokoyo.
Doso-shin (traveler's guardian deity) stone statue, small shrines, and jizo (the Guardian Deity of Travelers and Children) located at a roadside leading to a village means not only invocation of a deity for safety travel but also a barrier against tokoyo.
The meaning of tokoyo (eternal night) was assumed to be associated with a border between day and night like evening, and it was called 'Omagadoki' (twilight hour). Midnight, especially a deeply silent night, was considered to be overlapping tokoyo, so the time when something mysterious that did not exist in reality appeared was called 'ushimitsudoki' (the third quarter of the hour of the Ox (approximately 3:00 to 3:30 a.m.)), and people were afraid of the time.
== Ryukyu mythology == The Ryukyu mythology has Niraikanai (paradise across the ocean [folk belief of Okinawa & Amami]) as a similar idea of different world. == Others ==
* Japanese place names ** [[Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]][[Kitakata, Fukushima|Kitakata City]]Shiokawa-cho, Tsuneyo ** [[Higashishirakawa District, Fukushima|Higashishirakawa-gun]][[Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa Town]]Tsuneyo Kitano ** Tsuneze Nakano, Hanawa Town, Higashishirakawa District, Fukushima * A Japanese family name and surname. Tsuneze clan. Descendants of [[China (region)|China]]-derived Tsuneze [[Muraji|ren]] (Akazome clan), in the [[Aizu Domain|Aizu clan]] in the Edo period. Tsuneze Nagatane, etc. * [[Tsuneze Gihime Shrine]] - A shrine dedicated to the goddess Tsuneze Gihime. * Another name for the ancient Chinese state of "[[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan Kingdom]]".

The world is an important side of an [[antinomy]] in the [[Japanese Mythology]], the Ancient [[Shinto]], and Shinto religion, while the opposite is '[[Draft:This world|utsushiyo]]' (land of the living).<ref>{{Cite web |title=常世 (とこよ) - さくら葬祭 |url=https://sakurasousai.jp/dict_info/ta-007/#:~:text=%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%EF%BC%88%E3%81%A8%E3%81%93%E3%82%88%EF%BC%89%E3%81%A8%E3%81%AF,%E5%B8%B8%E5%A4%9C%E3%80%8D%E3%81%A8%E3%82%82%E8%A1%A8%E8%A8%98%E3%81%97%E3%81%9F%E3%80%82 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sakurasousai.jp}}</ref>
== Overview == Tokoyo' is a world which is always in the dead of the night<ref>{{Cite web |title=隠世（かくりよ） - 零 濡鴉の巫女 |url=https://sites.google.com/site/zeronurekarasu/yong-yu/yin-shi-kakuriyo |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sites.google.com}}</ref>, and it is sometimes identified with the world of the dead and yominokuni (realm of the dead) from the meaning of the [[kanji]] of tokoyo (常夜)<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典 |first=精選版 |title=隠世とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9A%A0%E4%B8%96-2021028 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>. However, after the release of Shinobu ORIKUCHI's thesis, "Haha ga kuni he/ Tokoyo he" (published in 1920), it does not a mere world of the dead, but a Utopia which is considered to exist over the sea or in the sea when you mentioned especially 'Tokoyo.' It was defined as "a strange land" where you are given a fortune, knowledge, a life, a long life, and eternal youth and immortality by a visit of marebito (a god which gives people his blessing and leaves).
In the ancient Shinto, it refers to the holy precincts or a world which is different from the real world and located beyond the mountains, the seas, woods, rivers, huge trees, or megalith such as [[himorogi]] (a temporarily erected sacred space or "altar" used as a locus of worship), [[Iwakura rock|iwakura]] (dwelling place of a god, usually in reference to a large rock), and so on where 'the aspect of the place' changes.
== The Japanese mythology == According to "[[Kojiki]]" (The Records of Ancient Matters) and the sixth alternate writing of "[[Nihonshoki]]," [[Sukunabikona]], who built up a country along with [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi no mikoto]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region), went to tokoyo, which was across the seas, after he finished creating the country. It has changed its meaning, and now it is said that tokoyo no kuni means the land of the dead or yomi (world after death).
In "[[Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]]" (Collection of Ten Thousand Leaves), the Palace of the Dragon King where Urashimataro visited was written as tokoyo, where time passed very differently from the real world. From this, it is said to mean a paradise of eternal youth and immortality.
According to an oracle told from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]] (the Sun Goddess) to Yamatohime no mikoto in "[[Nihonshoki]]," Ise is a province where the wave of tokoyo comes in.
== The ancient Shinto/the bounds of a sacred place, and kinsokuchi (tabooed land) == A megalith/spiritual rock, [[shinboku]] (sacred tree), [[Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-Mori]] (Sacred Shrine Forest), etc. which were considered [[yorishiro]] (spiritualistic mediums) in the ancient Shinto, indicate 'border' respectively because '籬' in himorogi (神籬) means 'a fence' and iwakura (磐座) is also written as '磐境' (rock border).
The place beyond the border is considered a sacred area, or tokoyo, and Okinoshima Island is considered sacred as a whole, including Chinju-no-Mori and kinsokuchi. Hedges of evergreen broad-leaved trees in Chinju-no-Mori and shrines are the bounds of a sacred place as well as the border of tokoyo. There are many forbidden places because people are trying not to invite a trouble or disaster caused by various things easily coming and going between utsushiyo and tokoyo.
Doso-shin (traveler's guardian deity) stone statue, small shrines, and jizo (the Guardian Deity of Travelers and Children) located at a roadside leading to a village means not only invocation of a deity for safety travel but also a barrier against tokoyo.
The meaning of tokoyo (eternal night) was assumed to be associated with a border between day and night like evening, and it was called 'Omagadoki' (twilight hour). Midnight, especially a deeply silent night, was considered to be overlapping tokoyo, so the time when something mysterious that did not exist in reality appeared was called 'ushimitsudoki' (the third quarter of the hour of the Ox (approximately 3:00 to 3:30 a.m.)), and people were afraid of the time.
== Ryukyu mythology == The Ryukyu mythology has Niraikanai (paradise across the ocean [folk belief of Okinawa & Amami]) as a similar idea of different world. == Others ==
* Japanese place names ** [[Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]][[Kitakata, Fukushima|Kitakata City]]Shiokawa-cho, Tsuneyo ** [[Higashishirakawa District, Fukushima|Higashishirakawa-gun]][[Hanawa, Fukushima|Hanawa Town]]Tsuneyo Kitano ** Tsuneze Nakano, Hanawa Town, Higashishirakawa District, Fukushima * A Japanese family name and surname. Tsuneze clan. Descendants of [[China (region)|China]]-derived Tsuneze [[Muraji|ren]] (Akazome clan), in the [[Aizu Domain|Aizu clan]] in the Edo period. Tsuneze Nagatane, etc. * [[Tsuneze Gihime Shrine]] - A shrine dedicated to the goddess Tsuneze Gihime. * Another name for the ancient Chinese state of "[[Yan Kingdom (Han dynasty)|Yan Kingdom]]".

(It is a world without any changes, especially a steady world without the law of cause and effect or a world which can be said to have no time base in a certain part.) It means the world after the death or 'eternity,' and it was also written in Chinese characters as '常夜' (eternal night) in ancient times. It means the world after the death or 'eternity,' and it was also written in Chinese characters as '常夜' (eternal night) in ancient times. The world is an important side of an [[antinomy]] in the [[Japanese Mythology]], the Ancient [[Shinto]], and Shinto religion, while the opposite is '[[Draft:This world|utsushiyo]]' (land of the living).<ref>{{Cite web |title=常世 (とこよ) - さくら葬祭 |url=https://sakurasousai.jp/dict_info/ta-007/#:~:text=%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%EF%BC%88%E3%81%A8%E3%81%93%E3%82%88%EF%BC%89%E3%81%A8%E3%81%AF,%E5%B8%B8%E5%A4%9C%E3%80%8D%E3%81%A8%E3%82%82%E8%A1%A8%E8%A8%98%E3%81%97%E3%81%9F%E3%80%82 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sakurasousai.jp}}</ref>
== Overview == Tokoyo' is a world which is always in the dead of the night<ref>{{Cite web |title=隠世（かくりよ） - 零 濡鴉の巫女 |url=https://sites.google.com/site/zeronurekarasu/yong-yu/yin-shi-kakuriyo |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sites.google.com}}</ref>, and it is sometimes identified with the world of the dead and yominokuni (realm of the dead) from the meaning of the [[kanji]] of tokoyo (常夜)<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典 |first=精選版 |title=隠世とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9A%A0%E4%B8%96-2021028 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>. However, after the release of Shinobu ORIKUCHI's thesis, "Haha ga kuni he/ Tokoyo he" (published in 1920), it does not a mere world of the dead, but a Utopia which is considered to exist over the sea or in the sea when you mentioned especially 'Tokoyo.' It was defined as "a strange land" where you are given a fortune, knowledge, a life, a long life, and eternal youth and immortality by a visit of marebito (a god which gives people his blessing and leaves).
It is seen as a world parallel to the human world but not worse<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.ph/wip/9hRmR |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
In [[Ko-Shintō]], it refers to the holy precincts or a world which is different from the real world and located beyond the mountains, the seas, woods, rivers, huge trees, or megalith such as [[himorogi]] (a temporarily erected sacred space or "altar" used as a locus of worship), [[Iwakura rock|iwakura]] (dwelling place of a god, usually in reference to a large rock), and so on where 'the aspect of the place' changes.
== The Japanese mythology == According to "[[Kojiki]]" (The Records of Ancient Matters) and the sixth alternate writing of "[[Nihonshoki]]," [[Sukunabikona]], who built up a country along with [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi no mikoto]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region), went to tokoyo, which was across the seas, after he finished creating the country. It has changed its meaning, and now it is said that tokoyo no kuni means the land of the dead or yomi (world after death).
In "[[Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]]" (Collection of Ten Thousand Leaves), the Palace of the Dragon King where Urashimataro visited was written as tokoyo, where time passed very differently from the real world. From this, it is said to mean a paradise of eternal youth and immortality.
According to an oracle told from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]] (the Sun Goddess) to Yamatohime no mikoto in "[[Nihonshoki]]," Ise is a province where the wave of tokoyo comes in.
== The ancient Shinto/the bounds of a sacred place, and kinsokuchi (tabooed land) == A megalith/spiritual rock, [[shinboku]] (sacred tree), [[Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-Mori]] (Sacred Shrine Forest), etc. which were considered [[yorishiro]] (spiritualistic mediums) in the ancient Shinto, indicate 'border' respectively because '籬' in himorogi (神籬) means 'a fence' and iwakura (磐座) is also written as '磐境' (rock border).
The place beyond the border is considered a sacred area, or tokoyo, and Okinoshima Island is considered sacred as a whole, including Chinju-no-Mori and kinsokuchi. Hedges of evergreen broad-leaved trees in Chinju-no-Mori and shrines are the bounds of a sacred place as well as the border of tokoyo. There are many forbidden places because people are trying not to invite a trouble or disaster caused by various things easily coming and going between utsushiyo and tokoyo.
Doso-shin (traveler's guardian deity) stone statue, small shrines, and jizo (the Guardian Deity of Travelers and Children) located at a roadside leading to a village means not only invocation of a deity for safety travel but also a barrier against tokoyo.
The meaning of tokoyo (eternal night) was assumed to be associated with a border between day and night like evening, and it was called 'Omagadoki' (twilight hour). Midnight, especially a deeply silent night, was considered to be overlapping tokoyo, so the time when something mysterious that did not exist in reality appeared was called 'ushimitsudoki' (the third quarter of the hour of the Ox (approximately 3:00 to 3:30 a.m.)), and people were afraid of the time.
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Tokoyo no Kuni|ja|常世の国}} * {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}} * [[Draft:This world|This world]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shimenawa]] * [[Bai sema]] * [[Otherworld]] * [[Amatsukami and Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Afterlife places]] [[Category:Mythological places]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]]
== References ==

It means the world after the death or 'eternity,' and it was also written in Chinese characters as '常夜' (eternal night) in ancient times. The world is an important side of an [[antinomy]] in the [[Japanese Mythology]], the Ancient [[Shinto]], and Shinto religion, while the opposite is '[[Draft:This world|utsushiyo]]' (land of the living).<ref>{{Cite web |title=常世 (とこよ) - さくら葬祭 |url=https://sakurasousai.jp/dict_info/ta-007/#:~:text=%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%EF%BC%88%E3%81%A8%E3%81%93%E3%82%88%EF%BC%89%E3%81%A8%E3%81%AF,%E5%B8%B8%E5%A4%9C%E3%80%8D%E3%81%A8%E3%82%82%E8%A1%A8%E8%A8%98%E3%81%97%E3%81%9F%E3%80%82 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sakurasousai.jp}}</ref>
== Overview == Tokoyo' is a world which is always in the dead of the night<ref>{{Cite web |title=隠世（かくりよ） - 零 濡鴉の巫女 |url=https://sites.google.com/site/zeronurekarasu/yong-yu/yin-shi-kakuriyo |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sites.google.com}}</ref>, and it is sometimes identified with the world of the dead and yominokuni (realm of the dead) from the meaning of the [[kanji]] of tokoyo (常夜)<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典 |first=精選版 |title=隠世とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9A%A0%E4%B8%96-2021028 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>. However, after the release of Shinobu ORIKUCHI's thesis, "Haha ga kuni he/ Tokoyo he" (published in 1920), it does not a mere world of the dead, but a Utopia which is considered to exist over the sea or in the sea when you mentioned especially 'Tokoyo.' It was defined as "a strange land" where you are given a fortune, knowledge, a life, a long life, and eternal youth and immortality by a visit of marebito (a god which gives people his blessing and leaves).
It is seen as a world parallel to the human world but not worse<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.ph/wip/9hRmR |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
In [[Ko-Shintō]], it refers to the holy precincts or a world which is different from the real world and located beyond the mountains, the seas, woods, rivers, huge trees, or megalith such as [[himorogi]] (a temporarily erected sacred space or "altar" used as a locus of worship), [[Iwakura rock|iwakura]] (dwelling place of a god, usually in reference to a large rock), and so on where 'the aspect of the place' changes.
== The Japanese mythology == According to "[[Kojiki]]" (The Records of Ancient Matters) and the sixth alternate writing of "[[Nihonshoki]]," [[Sukunabikona]], who built up a country along with [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi no mikoto]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region), went to tokoyo, which was across the seas, after he finished creating the country. It has changed its meaning, and now it is said that tokoyo no kuni means the land of the dead or yomi (world after death).
In "[[Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]]" (Collection of Ten Thousand Leaves), the Palace of the Dragon King where Urashimataro visited was written as tokoyo, where time passed very differently from the real world. From this, it is said to mean a paradise of eternal youth and immortality.
According to an oracle told from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]] (the Sun Goddess) to Yamatohime no mikoto in "[[Nihonshoki]]," Ise is a province where the wave of tokoyo comes in.
== The ancient Shinto/the bounds of a sacred place, and kinsokuchi (tabooed land) == A megalith/spiritual rock, [[shinboku]] (sacred tree), [[Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-Mori]] (Sacred Shrine Forest), etc. which were considered [[yorishiro]] (spiritualistic mediums) in the ancient Shinto, indicate 'border' respectively because '籬' in himorogi (神籬) means 'a fence' and iwakura (磐座) is also written as '磐境' (rock border).
The place beyond the border is considered a sacred area, or tokoyo, and Okinoshima Island is considered sacred as a whole, including Chinju-no-Mori and kinsokuchi. Hedges of evergreen broad-leaved trees in Chinju-no-Mori and shrines are the bounds of a sacred place as well as the border of tokoyo. There are many forbidden places because people are trying not to invite a trouble or disaster caused by various things easily coming and going between utsushiyo and tokoyo.
Doso-shin (traveler's guardian deity) stone statue, small shrines, and jizo (the Guardian Deity of Travelers and Children) located at a roadside leading to a village means not only invocation of a deity for safety travel but also a barrier against tokoyo.
The meaning of tokoyo (eternal night) was assumed to be associated with a border between day and night like evening, and it was called 'Omagadoki' (twilight hour). Midnight, especially a deeply silent night, was considered to be overlapping tokoyo, so the time when something mysterious that did not exist in reality appeared was called 'ushimitsudoki' (the third quarter of the hour of the Ox (approximately 3:00 to 3:30 a.m.)), and people were afraid of the time.
== See also ==
* {{ill|Tokoyo no Kuni|ja|常世の国}} * {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}} * [[Draft:This world|This world]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shimenawa]] * [[Bai sema]] * [[Otherworld]] * [[Amatsukami and Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Afterlife places]] [[Category:Mythological places]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

It means the world after the death or 'eternity,' and it was also written in Chinese characters as '常夜' (eternal night) in ancient times. The world is an important side of an [[antinomy]] in the [[Japanese Mythology]], the Ancient [[Shinto]], and Shinto religion, while the opposite is '[[This world|utsushiyo]]' (land of the living).<ref>{{Cite web |title=常世 (とこよ) - さくら葬祭 |url=https://sakurasousai.jp/dict_info/ta-007/#:~:text=%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%EF%BC%88%E3%81%A8%E3%81%93%E3%82%88%EF%BC%89%E3%81%A8%E3%81%AF,%E5%B8%B8%E5%A4%9C%E3%80%8D%E3%81%A8%E3%82%82%E8%A1%A8%E8%A8%98%E3%81%97%E3%81%9F%E3%80%82 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sakurasousai.jp}}</ref>
== Overview == Tokoyo' is a world which is always in the dead of the night<ref>{{Cite web |title=隠世（かくりよ） - 零 濡鴉の巫女 |url=https://sites.google.com/site/zeronurekarasu/yong-yu/yin-shi-kakuriyo |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sites.google.com}}</ref>, and it is sometimes identified with the world of the dead and yominokuni (realm of the dead) from the meaning of the [[kanji]] of tokoyo (常夜)<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典 |first=精選版 |title=隠世とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9A%A0%E4%B8%96-2021028 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>. However, after the release of Shinobu ORIKUCHI's thesis, "Haha ga kuni he/ Tokoyo he" (published in 1920), it does not a mere world of the dead, but a Utopia which is considered to exist over the sea or in the sea when you mentioned especially 'Tokoyo.' It was defined as "a strange land" where you are given a fortune, knowledge, a life, a long life, and eternal youth and immortality by a visit of marebito (a god which gives people his blessing and leaves).
It is seen as a world parallel to the human world but not worse<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.ph/wip/9hRmR |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
In [[Ko-Shintō]], it refers to the holy precincts or a world which is different from the real world and located beyond the mountains, the seas, woods, rivers, huge trees, or megalith such as [[himorogi]] (a temporarily erected sacred space or "altar" used as a locus of worship), [[Iwakura rock|iwakura]] (dwelling place of a god, usually in reference to a large rock), and so on where 'the aspect of the place' changes.
== The Japanese mythology == According to "[[Kojiki]]" (The Records of Ancient Matters) and the sixth alternate writing of "[[Nihonshoki]]," [[Sukunabikona]], who built up a country along with [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi no mikoto]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region), went to tokoyo, which was across the seas, after he finished creating the country. It has changed its meaning, and now it is said that tokoyo no kuni means the land of the dead or yomi (world after death).
In "[[Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]]" (Collection of Ten Thousand Leaves), the Palace of the Dragon King where Urashimataro visited was written as tokoyo, where time passed very differently from the real world. From this, it is said to mean a paradise of eternal youth and immortality.
According to an oracle told from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]] (the Sun Goddess) to Yamatohime no mikoto in "[[Nihonshoki]]," Ise is a province where the wave of tokoyo comes in.
== The ancient Shinto/the bounds of a sacred place, and kinsokuchi (tabooed land) == A megalith/spiritual rock, [[shinboku]] (sacred tree), [[Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-Mori]] (Sacred Shrine Forest), etc. which were considered [[yorishiro]] (spiritualistic mediums) in the ancient Shinto, indicate 'border' respectively because '籬' in himorogi (神籬) means 'a fence' and iwakura (磐座) is also written as '磐境' (rock border).
The place beyond the border is considered a sacred area, or tokoyo, and Okinoshima Island is considered sacred as a whole, including Chinju-no-Mori and kinsokuchi. Hedges of evergreen broad-leaved trees in Chinju-no-Mori and shrines are the bounds of a sacred place as well as the border of tokoyo. There are many forbidden places because people are trying not to invite a trouble or disaster caused by various things easily coming and going between utsushiyo and tokoyo.
Doso-shin (traveler's guardian deity) stone statue, small shrines, and jizo (the Guardian Deity of Travelers and Children) located at a roadside leading to a village means not only invocation of a deity for safety travel but also a barrier against tokoyo.
The meaning of tokoyo (eternal night) was assumed to be associated with a border between day and night like evening, and it was called 'Omagadoki' (twilight hour). Midnight, especially a deeply silent night, was considered to be overlapping tokoyo, so the time when something mysterious that did not exist in reality appeared was called 'ushimitsudoki' (the third quarter of the hour of the Ox (approximately 3:00 to 3:30 a.m.)), and people were afraid of the time.
== See also ==
* {{ill|Tokoyo no Kuni|ja|常世の国}} * {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}} * [[This world]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shimenawa]] * [[Bai sema]] * [[Otherworld]] * [[Amatsukami and Kunitsukami]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
[[Category:Afterlife places]] [[Category:Mythological places]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]]

It means the world after the death or 'eternity,' and it was also written in Chinese characters as '常夜' (eternal night) in ancient times. The world is an important side of an [[antinomy]] in the [[Japanese Mythology]], the Ancient [[Shinto]], and Shinto religion, while the opposite is '[[This world|utsushiyo]]' (land of the living).<ref>{{Cite web |title=常世 (とこよ) - さくら葬祭 |url=https://sakurasousai.jp/dict_info/ta-007/#:~:text=%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%EF%BC%88%E3%81%A8%E3%81%93%E3%82%88%EF%BC%89%E3%81%A8%E3%81%AF,%E5%B8%B8%E5%A4%9C%E3%80%8D%E3%81%A8%E3%82%82%E8%A1%A8%E8%A8%98%E3%81%97%E3%81%9F%E3%80%82 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sakurasousai.jp}}</ref>
== Overview == Tokoyo' is a world which is always in the dead of the night,<ref>{{Cite web |title=隠世（かくりよ） - 零 濡鴉の巫女 |url=https://sites.google.com/site/zeronurekarasu/yong-yu/yin-shi-kakuriyo |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sites.google.com}}</ref> and it is sometimes identified with the world of the dead and yominokuni (realm of the dead) from the meaning of the [[kanji]] of tokoyo (常夜).<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典 |first=精選版 |title=隠世とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9A%A0%E4%B8%96-2021028 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> However, after the release of Shinobu ORIKUCHI's thesis, "Haha ga kuni he/ Tokoyo he" (published in 1920), it does not a mere world of the dead, but a Utopia which is considered to exist over the sea or in the sea when you mentioned especially 'Tokoyo.' It was defined as "a strange land" where you are given a fortune, knowledge, a life, a long life, and eternal youth and immortality by a visit of marebito (a god which gives people his blessing and leaves).
It is seen as a world parallel to the human world but not worse<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://archive.ph/wip/9hRmR |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
In [[Ko-Shintō]], it refers to the holy precincts or a world which is different from the real world and located beyond the mountains, the seas, woods, rivers, huge trees, or megalith such as [[himorogi]] (a temporarily erected sacred space or "altar" used as a locus of worship), [[Iwakura rock|iwakura]] (dwelling place of a god, usually in reference to a large rock), and so on where 'the aspect of the place' changes.
== The Japanese mythology == According to "[[Kojiki]]" (The Records of Ancient Matters) and the sixth alternate writing of "[[Nihonshoki]]," [[Sukunabikona]], who built up a country along with [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi no mikoto]] (chief god of Izumo in southern Honshu Island, Japan, and the central character in the important cycle of myths set in that region), went to tokoyo, which was across the seas, after he finished creating the country. It has changed its meaning, and now it is said that tokoyo no kuni means the land of the dead or yomi (world after death).
In "[[Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]]" (Collection of Ten Thousand Leaves), the Palace of the Dragon King where Urashimataro visited was written as tokoyo, where time passed very differently from the real world. From this, it is said to mean a paradise of eternal youth and immortality.
According to an oracle told from [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]] (the Sun Goddess) to Yamatohime no mikoto in "[[Nihonshoki]]," Ise is a province where the wave of tokoyo comes in.
== The ancient Shinto/the bounds of a sacred place, and kinsokuchi (tabooed land) == A megalith/spiritual rock, [[shinboku]] (sacred tree), [[Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-Mori]] (Sacred Shrine Forest), etc. which were considered [[yorishiro]] (spiritualistic mediums) in the ancient Shinto, indicate 'border' respectively because '籬' in himorogi (神籬) means 'a fence' and iwakura (磐座) is also written as '磐境' (rock border).
The place beyond the border is considered a sacred area, or tokoyo, and Okinoshima Island is considered sacred as a whole, including Chinju-no-Mori and kinsokuchi. Hedges of evergreen broad-leaved trees in Chinju-no-Mori and shrines are the bounds of a sacred place as well as the border of tokoyo. There are many forbidden places because people are trying not to invite a trouble or disaster caused by various things easily coming and going between utsushiyo and tokoyo.
Doso-shin (traveler's guardian deity) stone statue, small shrines, and jizo (the Guardian Deity of Travelers and Children) located at a roadside leading to a village means not only invocation of a deity for safety travel but also a barrier against tokoyo.
The meaning of tokoyo (eternal night) was assumed to be associated with a border between day and night like evening, and it was called 'Omagadoki' (twilight hour). Midnight, especially a deeply silent night, was considered to be overlapping tokoyo, so the time when something mysterious that did not exist in reality appeared was called 'ushimitsudoki' (the third quarter of the hour of the Ox (approximately 3:00 to 3:30 a.m.)), and people were afraid of the time.
== See also ==
* {{ill|Tokoyo no Kuni|ja|常世の国}} * {{ill|Tokoyo clan|ja|常世氏}} * [[This world]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shimenawa]] * [[Bai sema]] * [[Otherworld]] * [[Amatsukami and Kunitsukami]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{AfC submission|t||ts=20221229125423|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Everlasting_world]] before draftification: *[[Tokoyo Yomi]] *[[Draft:Tokoyo]] *[[Draft:Everlasting world 2]] *[[Tokoyo and Kakuriyo]] *[[Draft:Everlasting 2]] *[[Tokoyo (eternal night)]] *[[Unseen world]] *[[Draft:Tokoyo Yomi]] *[[Draft:Tokoyo and Kakuriyo]] *[[Draft:Tokoyo (eternal night)]] --> {{refimprove|date=December 2022}}
The {{nihongo|Everlasting world|常世|{{ill|Unseen world|lt=Tokoyo|ja|常世}}}}, also known as {{nihongo|Unseen world|隠世、幽世|Kakuriyo}},<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=隠り世(かくりよ)と現世（うつしよ）、隠り世ってどんなところ？ - waqwaq |url=https://waqwaq-j.com/japan/1093/ |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=waqwaq-j.com}}</ref> is a world in [[shinto]] which never changes. (It is a world without any changes, especially a steady world without the law of cause and effect or a world which can be said to have no time base in a certain part.) It means the world after the death or 'eternity,' and it was also written in Chinese characters as '常夜' (eternal night) in ancient times. The world is an important side of an [[antinomy]] in the [[Japanese Mythology]], the Ancient [[Shinto]], and Shinto religion, while the opposite is '[[This world|utsushiyo]]' (land of the living).<ref>{{Cite web |title=常世 (とこよ) - さくら葬祭 |url=https://sakurasousai.jp/dict_info/ta-007/#:~:text=%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%EF%BC%88%E3%81%A8%E3%81%93%E3%82%88%EF%BC%89%E3%81%A8%E3%81%AF,%E5%B8%B8%E5%A4%9C%E3%80%8D%E3%81%A8%E3%82%82%E8%A1%A8%E8%A8%98%E3%81%97%E3%81%9F%E3%80%82 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sakurasousai.jp}}</ref>
In Shintoism and Japanese mythology, there is a concept known as "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei" (also translated as "hidden world") which is believed to be the afterlife. It is often contrasted with the present world, known as "Utsushiyo" or "Gense", and is thought to be a paradise or "ideal world" where ancestors, friends, and loved ones who have passed away reside. It can also be written as "Tokoyo" or "Tokoyoru" which refers to eternal world or eternal night.<ref name=":1" />
The concept of "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei" is a central belief in ancient Shintoism, where it is referred to as a realm of the gods that is eternal and unchanging. It is also mentioned in ancient Japanese texts such as "Kojiki" and "Nihon Shoki", where it is described as a place where the god Sukuna-biko-no-kami, who helped create the country, goes after completing his mission. In Folklore studies, it is defined as a place where the deceased or spirits, creates an ideal land beyond the sea.<ref name=":1" />
In contrast, "Tokoyo" or "Tokoyoru" is often associated with negative connotations such as hell or the land of the dead.<ref name=":1" />
In summary, "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei" is a belief in the afterlife in Shintoism and Japanese mythology, where it is believed to be a paradise or "ideal world" where ancestors, friends, and loved ones who have passed away reside, in contrast to the present world. It is also known as "Tokoyo" or "Tokoyoru" which refers to eternal world or eternal night.<ref name=":1" />
== Overview == Tokoyo' is a world which is always in the dead of the night,<ref>{{Cite web |title=隠世（かくりよ） - 零 濡鴉の巫女 |url=https://sites.google.com/site/zeronurekarasu/yong-yu/yin-shi-kakuriyo |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sites.google.com}}</ref> and it is sometimes identified with the world of the dead and yominokuni (realm of the dead) from the meaning of the [[kanji]] of tokoyo (常夜).<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典 |first=精選版 |title=隠世とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9A%A0%E4%B8%96-2021028 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> However, after the release of Shinobu ORIKUCHI's thesis, "Haha ga kuni he/ Tokoyo he" (published in 1920), it does not a mere world of the dead, but a Utopia which is considered to exist over the sea or in the sea when you mentioned especially 'Tokoyo.' It was defined as "a strange land" where you are given a fortune, knowledge, a life, a long life, and eternal youth and immortality by a visit of marebito (a god which gives people his blessing and leaves).
It is seen as a world parallel to the human world but not worse<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.ph/wip/9hRmR |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
However, "Yuusei" is believed to have stronger connotations of the "Tokoyo" as a paradise, or "ideal world". The kanji for "Yuusei" can also be written as "Kakuriyo" and can also be translated as "hidden world". The "Yuusei" is not in stark opposition to the "Utsushiyo", but rather exists in parallel to it, and it is suggested that it is a place where ancestors, friends, and loved ones who have passed away reside.<ref name=":0" />
== See also ==

== References == {{Reflist}}

The {{nihongo|Everlasting world|常世|{{ill|Unseen world|lt=Tokoyo|ja|常世}}}}, also known as {{nihongo|Unseen world|隠世、幽世|Kakuriyo}},<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=隠り世(かくりよ)と現世（うつしよ）、隠り世ってどんなところ？ - waqwaq |url=https://waqwaq-j.com/japan/1093/ |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=waqwaq-j.com}}</ref> is a world in [[shinto]] which never changes. (It is a world without any changes, especially a steady world without the law of cause and effect or a world which can be said to have no time base in a certain part.) It means the world after the death or 'eternity,' and it was also written in Chinese characters as '常夜' (eternal night) in ancient times. The world is an important side of an [[antinomy]] in the [[Japanese Mythology]], the Ancient [[Shinto]], and Shinto religion, while the opposite is '[[This world|utsushiyo]]' (land of the living).<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=常世 (とこよ) - さくら葬祭 |url=https://sakurasousai.jp/dict_info/ta-007/#:~:text=%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%EF%BC%88%E3%81%A8%E3%81%93%E3%82%88%EF%BC%89%E3%81%A8%E3%81%AF,%E5%B8%B8%E5%A4%9C%E3%80%8D%E3%81%A8%E3%82%82%E8%A1%A8%E8%A8%98%E3%81%97%E3%81%9F%E3%80%82 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sakurasousai.jp}}</ref>
In Shintoism and Japanese mythology, there is a concept known as "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei" (also translated as "hidden world") which is believed to be the afterlife. It is often contrasted with the present world, known as "Utsushiyo" or "Gense", and is thought to be a paradise or "ideal world" where ancestors, friends, and loved ones who have passed away reside. It can also be written as "Tokoyo" or "Tokoyoru" which refers to eternal world or eternal night.<ref name=":1" />
The concept of "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei" is a central belief in ancient Shintoism, where it is referred to as a realm of the gods that is eternal and unchanging. It is also mentioned in ancient Japanese texts such as "Kojiki" and "Nihon Shoki", where it is described as a place where the god Sukuna-biko-no-kami, who helped create the country, goes after completing his mission. In Folklore studies, it is defined as a place where the deceased or spirits, creates an ideal land beyond the sea.<ref name=":1" />
In contrast, "Tokoyo" or "Tokoyoru" is often associated with negative connotations such as hell or the land of the dead.<ref name=":1" />
In summary, "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei" is a belief in the afterlife in Shintoism and Japanese mythology, where it is believed to be a paradise or "ideal world" where ancestors, friends, and loved ones who have passed away reside, in contrast to the present world. It is also known as "Tokoyo" or "Tokoyoru" which refers to eternal world or eternal night.<ref name=":1" />
"Tokoyo" (also referred to as "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei") is a concept in Japanese mythology and Shintoism that refers to an eternal and unchanging world that is separate from the mortal world. It is often thought of as a paradise or "ideal world" where ancestors, friends, and loved ones who have passed away reside. It is also referred to as the "world of the gods" and is one of the important dualistic world views in Shintoism.<ref name=":2" />
"Tokoyo" is also known as "Tokoyoru" which refers to eternal night, and is sometimes associated with negative connotations such as hell or the land of the dead. However, according to the Folklore studies, it is defined as a place where the deceased or spirits, creates an ideal land beyond the sea.<ref name=":2" />
In ancient Shintoism and Japanese texts, "Tokoyo" is described as a place located beyond mountains, seas, forests, rivers, and giant rocks. It is often described as a place where the god Sukuna-biko-no-kami, who helped create the country, goes after completing his mission. In addition, it is said that this world is connected to different spaces through the boundaries of different landscapes such as deep sea, mountain<ref name=":2" />
== Overview == Tokoyo' is a world which is always in the dead of the night,<ref>{{Cite web |title=隠世（かくりよ） - 零 濡鴉の巫女 |url=https://sites.google.com/site/zeronurekarasu/yong-yu/yin-shi-kakuriyo |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sites.google.com}}</ref> and it is sometimes identified with the world of the dead and yominokuni (realm of the dead) from the meaning of the [[kanji]] of tokoyo (常夜).<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典 |first=精選版 |title=隠世とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9A%A0%E4%B8%96-2021028 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> However, after the release of Shinobu ORIKUCHI's thesis, "Haha ga kuni he/ Tokoyo he" (published in 1920), it does not a mere world of the dead, but a Utopia which is considered to exist over the sea or in the sea when you mentioned especially 'Tokoyo.' It was defined as "a strange land" where you are given a fortune, knowledge, a life, a long life, and eternal youth and immortality by a visit of marebito (a god which gives people his blessing and leaves).
It is seen as a world parallel to the human world but not worse<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.ph/wip/9hRmR |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
However, "Yuusei" is believed to have stronger connotations of the "Tokoyo" as a paradise, or "ideal world". The kanji for "Yuusei" can also be written as "Kakuriyo" and can also be translated as "hidden world". The "Yuusei" is not in stark opposition to the "Utsushiyo", but rather exists in parallel to it, and it is suggested that it is a place where ancestors, friends, and loved ones who have passed away reside.<ref name=":0" />

This association with eternal darkness has led to the belief that the boundary between the world of the living and the world of the dead is a place of constant twilight, and it is said that it is easier to get lost in "Kakuriyo" during dusk. It is also considered as the world of spirits and afterlife, where the souls of the dead reside and where the consequences of actions of the living are embodied.<ref name=":3" />
== See also ==

{{AfC submission|t||ts=20221229125423|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}}
<!-- Note: The following pages were redirects to [[Everlasting_world]] before draftification: *[[Tokoyo Yomi]] *[[Draft:Tokoyo]] *[[Draft:Everlasting world 2]] *[[Tokoyo and Kakuriyo]] *[[Draft:Everlasting 2]] *[[Tokoyo (eternal night)]] *[[Unseen world]] *[[Draft:Tokoyo Yomi]] *[[Draft:Tokoyo and Kakuriyo]] *[[Draft:Tokoyo (eternal night)]] --> {{refimprove|date=December 2022}}
The {{nihongo|Everlasting world|常世|{{ill|Unseen world|lt=Tokoyo|ja|常世}}}}, also known as {{nihongo|Unseen world|隠世、幽世|Kakuriyo}},<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=隠り世(かくりよ)と現世（うつしよ）、隠り世ってどんなところ？ - waqwaq |url=https://waqwaq-j.com/japan/1093/ |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=waqwaq-j.com}}</ref> is a world in [[shinto]] which never changes<ref name=":1" />. (It is a world without any changes, especially a steady world without the law of cause and effect or a world which can be said to have no time base in a certain part.) It means the world after the death or 'eternity,' and it was also written in Chinese characters as '常夜' (eternal night) in ancient times. The world is an important side of an [[antinomy]] in the [[Japanese Mythology]], the Ancient [[Shinto]], and Shinto religion, while the opposite is '[[This world|utsushiyo]]' (land of the living).<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=常世 (とこよ) - さくら葬祭 |url=https://sakurasousai.jp/dict_info/ta-007/#:~:text=%E5%B8%B8%E4%B8%96%EF%BC%88%E3%81%A8%E3%81%93%E3%82%88%EF%BC%89%E3%81%A8%E3%81%AF,%E5%B8%B8%E5%A4%9C%E3%80%8D%E3%81%A8%E3%82%82%E8%A1%A8%E8%A8%98%E3%81%97%E3%81%9F%E3%80%82 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sakurasousai.jp}}</ref>
In Shintoism and Japanese mythology, there is a concept known as "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei" (also translated as "hidden world") which is believed to be the afterlife. It is often contrasted with the present world, known as "Utsushiyo" or "Gense", and is thought to be a paradise or "ideal world" where ancestors, friends, and loved ones who have passed away reside. It can also be written as "Tokoyo" or "Tokoyoru" which refers to eternal world or eternal night.<ref name=":1" />
The concept of "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei" is a central belief in ancient Shintoism, where it is referred to as a realm of the gods that is eternal and unchanging. It is also mentioned in ancient Japanese texts such as "Kojiki" and "Nihon Shoki", where it is described as a place where the god Sukuna-biko-no-kami, who helped create the country, goes after completing his mission. In Folklore studies, it is defined as a place where the deceased or spirits, creates an ideal land beyond the sea.<ref name=":1" />
In contrast, "Tokoyo" or "Tokoyoru" is often associated with negative connotations such as hell or the land of the dead.<ref name=":1" /> Tokoyo' is a world which is always in the dead of the night,<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=隠世（かくりよ） - 零 濡鴉の巫女 |url=https://sites.google.com/site/zeronurekarasu/yong-yu/yin-shi-kakuriyo |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=sites.google.com}}</ref> and it is sometimes identified with the world of the dead and yominokuni (realm of the dead) from the meaning of the [[kanji]] of tokoyo (常夜).<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典 |first=精選版 |title=隠世とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9A%A0%E4%B8%96-2021028 |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
In summary, "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei" is a belief in the afterlife in Shintoism and Japanese mythology, where it is believed to be a paradise or "ideal world" where ancestors, friends, and loved ones who have passed away reside, in contrast to the present world. It is also known as "Tokoyo" or "Tokoyoru" which refers to eternal world or eternal night.<ref name=":1" />
"Tokoyo" (also referred to as "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei") is a concept in Japanese mythology and Shintoism that refers to an eternal and unchanging world that is separate from the mortal world. It is often thought of as a paradise or "ideal world" where ancestors, friends, and loved ones who have passed away reside. It is also referred to as the "world of the gods" and is one of the important dualistic world views in Shintoism.<ref name=":2" />
"Tokoyo" is also known as "Tokoyoru" which refers to eternal night, and is sometimes associated with negative connotations such as hell or the land of the dead. However, according to the Folklore studies, it is defined as a place where the deceased or spirits, creates an ideal land beyond the sea.<ref name=":2" />
In ancient Shintoism and Japanese texts, "Tokoyo" is described as a place located beyond mountains, seas, forests, rivers, and giant rocks. It is often described as a place where the god Sukuna-biko-no-kami, who helped create the country, goes after completing his mission. In addition, it is said that this world is connected to different spaces through the boundaries of different landscapes such as deep sea, mountain<ref name=":2" />
However, "Yuusei" is believed to have stronger connotations of the "Tokoyo" as a paradise, or "ideal world". The kanji for "Yuusei" can also be written as "Kakuriyo" and can also be translated as "hidden world". The "Yuusei" is not in stark opposition to the "Utsushiyo", but rather exists in parallel to it, and it is suggested that it is a place where ancestors, friends, and loved ones who have passed away reside.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://archive.ph/wip/9hRmR |access-date=2022-12-22 |website=archive.ph}}</ref>
In many cultures and religions, the concept of "the afterlife" refers to a realm separate from the world of the living. In Shintoism, this realm is known as "Kakuriyo" or "Yuusei" (translated as "hidden world" or "world of the dead"). It is often thought of as an eternal and unchanging world, where the souls of the deceased reside.<ref name=":3" />
In Japanese mythology and Shintoism, "Kakuriyo" is often associated with the image of eternal night, as it is a world where the night never ends. This association with eternal darkness has led to the belief that the boundary between the world of the living and the world of the dead is a place of constant twilight, and it is said that it is easier to get lost in "Kakuriyo" during dusk. It is also considered as the world of spirits and afterlife, where the souls of the dead reside and where the consequences of actions of the living are embodied.<ref name=":3" />

revision.text:
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}
:
{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[eternal youth]]. [[Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />
[[Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />
Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />
== Myth of Okuninushi == {{Main|Ōkuninushi}} In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>
The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>
== Identification with Mount Penglai == {{Main|Mount Penglai}} In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>
The Asteroid [[162173 Ryugu]] has two [[Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>
== See also ==
* [[This world]] * [[Yorishiro]] * [[Shimenawa]] * [[Bai sema]] * [[Kunitsukami]]
== Notes == {{Notelist}}

{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}
:
{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[eternal youth]]. [[Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />
[[Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />
Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />
== Myth of Okuninushi == {{Main|Ōkuninushi}} In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>
The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>
== Identification with Mount Penglai == {{Main|Mount Penglai}} In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>
The Asteroid [[162173 Ryugu]] has two [[Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}
:
{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[eternal youth]]. [[Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />
[[Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />
Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />
== Myth of Okuninushi == {{Main|Ōkuninushi}} In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>
The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>
== Identification with Mount Penglai == {{Main|Mount Penglai}} In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>
The Asteroid [[162173 Ryugu]] has two [[Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[eternal youth]]. [[Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

In the reign of [[Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>
The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>
== Identification with Mount Penglai == {{Main|Mount Penglai}} In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>
The Asteroid [[162173 Ryugu]] has two [[Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>
