{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}
:
{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[eternal youth]]. [[Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />
[[Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />
Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />
== Myth of Okuninushi == {{Main|Ōkuninushi}} In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>
The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>
== Identification with Mount Penglai == {{Main|Mount Penglai}} In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>
The Asteroid [[162173 Ryugu]] has two [[Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

kuni]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokoyo no kuni]][[Category:11]]

{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}
:
{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[eternal youth]]. [[Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />
[[Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />
Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />
== Myth of Okuninushi == {{Main|Ōkuninushi}} In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>
The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>
== Identification with Mount Penglai == {{Main|Mount Penglai}} In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>
The Asteroid [[162173 Ryugu]] has two [[Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}
:
{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[eternal youth]]. [[Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />
[[Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />
Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />
== Myth of Okuninushi == {{Main|Ōkuninushi}} In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>
The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>
== Identification with Mount Penglai == {{Main|Mount Penglai}} In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>
The Asteroid [[162173 Ryugu]] has two [[Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[eternal youth]]. Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

[http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}
:
{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[eternal youth]]. [[Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />
[[Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />
Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />
== Myth of Okuninushi == {{Main|Ōkuninushi}} In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>
The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>
== Identification with Mount Penglai == {{Main|Mount Penglai}} In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>
The Asteroid [[162173 Ryugu]] has two [[Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11060259]]
Wikidata name: Tokoyo no Kuni
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[:en:Otherworld|Otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[:en:Afterlife|Afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[:en:Eternal youth|Eternal youth]]. [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

[[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid [[:en:162173 Ryugu|162173 Ryugu]] has two [[:en:Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

== See also ==

* [[:en:This world|This world]]
* [[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]]
* [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]
* [[:en:Bai sema|Bai sema]]
* [[Kunitsukami]]

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[:en:Otherworld|Otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[:en:Afterlife|Afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[:en:Eternal youth|Eternal youth]]. [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

[[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid [[:en:162173 Ryugu|162173 Ryugu]] has two [[:en:Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

Wikidata name: Tokoyo no Kuni
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[:en:Otherworld|Otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[:en:Afterlife|Afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[:en:Eternal youth|Eternal youth]]. [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

[[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid [[:en:162173 Ryugu|162173 Ryugu]] has two [[:en:Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]]

<!--unattributed in the printed book--><!--[[:ja:長谷川武次郎]], 弘文社--><!--国際日本文学研究集会会議録--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--成城大學經濟研究--><!--小林永濯 1843-1890--><!--彩流社--><!--4779113717, --><!--愛知淑徳大学論集一文学部・文学研究科篇--><!--比較文学--><!--|season=autumn-->

[[ja:常世の国]]
[[ko:토코요국]]
[[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Tokoyo]]
[[zh:常世國]]
{{wikidata link|Q11060259}}
[[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata has English label - based on Wikidata English label
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11060259]]
Wikidata name: Tokoyo no Kuni
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[:en:Otherworld|Otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[:en:Afterlife|Afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[:en:Eternal youth|Eternal youth]]. [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

[[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid [[:en:162173 Ryugu|162173 Ryugu]] has two [[:en:Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11060259]]
Wikidata name: Tokoyo no Kuni
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[:en:Otherworld|Otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[:en:Afterlife|Afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[:en:Eternal youth|Eternal youth]]. [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

[[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid [[:en:162173 Ryugu|162173 Ryugu]] has two [[:en:Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid [[:en:162173 Ryugu|162173 Ryugu]] has two [[:en:Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11060259]]
Wikidata name: Tokoyo no Kuni
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[:en:Otherworld|Otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[:en:Afterlife|Afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[:en:Eternal youth|Eternal youth]]. [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

[[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid [[:en:162173 Ryugu|162173 Ryugu]] has two [[:en:Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]]

<!--unattributed in the printed book--><!--[[:ja:長谷川武次郎]], 弘文社--><!--国際日本文学研究集会会議録--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--成城大學經濟研究--><!--小林永濯 1843-1890--><!--彩流社--><!--4779113717, --><!--愛知淑徳大学論集一文学部・文学研究科篇--><!--比較文学--><!--|season=autumn-->

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11060259]]
Wikidata name: Tokoyo no Kuni
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[:en:Otherworld|Otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[:en:Afterlife|Afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[:en:Eternal youth|Eternal youth]]. [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

[[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid [[:en:162173 Ryugu|162173 Ryugu]] has two [[:en:Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11060259]]
Wikidata name: Tokoyo no Kuni
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[:en:Otherworld|Otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[:en:Afterlife|Afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[:en:Eternal youth|Eternal youth]]. [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

[[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid [[:en:162173 Ryugu|162173 Ryugu]] has two [[:en:Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "[[:en:Otherworld|Otherworld]]" though not necessarily seen as a place in the [[:en:Afterlife|Afterlife]], but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with [[:en:Eternal youth|Eternal youth]]. [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

[[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-kuni]] is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of [[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]] travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

[http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with [[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]].{{efn|It is written as Horai ([[:en:Mount Penglai|Mount Penglai]]) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid [[:en:162173 Ryugu|162173 Ryugu]] has two [[:en:Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|fossae]] named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>
