revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11060259]]
Wikidata name: Tokoyo no Kuni
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "{{ill|Otherworld|en|Otherworld|WD=Q13383731|lt=Otherworld}}" though not necessarily seen as a place in the {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife}}, but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with {{ill|Eternal youth|en|Eternal youth|WD=Q836886|lt=Eternal youth}}. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}} categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni}} is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}} the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of {{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori}} travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}} to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}.{{efn|It is written as Horai ({{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid {{ill|162173 Ryugu|en|162173 Ryugu|WD=Q1385178|lt=162173 Ryugu}} has two {{ill|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|en|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|WD=Q1439394|lt=fossae}} named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11060259]]
Wikidata name: Tokoyo no Kuni
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "{{ill|Otherworld|en|Otherworld|WD=Q13383731|lt=Otherworld}}" though not necessarily seen as a place in the {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife}}, but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with {{ill|Eternal youth|en|Eternal youth|WD=Q836886|lt=Eternal youth}}. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}} categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni}} is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}} the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of {{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori}} travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}} to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}.{{efn|It is written as Horai ({{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid {{ill|162173 Ryugu|en|162173 Ryugu|WD=Q1385178|lt=162173 Ryugu}} has two {{ill|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|en|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|WD=Q1439394|lt=fossae}} named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11060259]]
Wikidata name: Tokoyo no Kuni
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "{{ill|Otherworld|en|Otherworld|WD=Q13383731|lt=Otherworld}}" though not necessarily seen as a place in the {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife}}, but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with {{ill|Eternal youth|en|Eternal youth|WD=Q836886|lt=Eternal youth}}. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}} categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni}} is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}} the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of {{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori}} travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}} to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}.{{efn|It is written as Horai ({{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid {{ill|162173 Ryugu|en|162173 Ryugu|WD=Q1385178|lt=162173 Ryugu}} has two {{ill|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|en|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|WD=Q1439394|lt=fossae}} named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}.{{efn|It is written as Horai ({{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid {{ill|162173 Ryugu|en|162173 Ryugu|WD=Q1385178|lt=162173 Ryugu}} has two {{ill|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|en|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|WD=Q1439394|lt=fossae}} named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11060259]]
Wikidata name: Tokoyo no Kuni
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "{{ill|Otherworld|en|Otherworld|WD=Q13383731|lt=Otherworld}}" though not necessarily seen as a place in the {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife}}, but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with {{ill|Eternal youth|en|Eternal youth|WD=Q836886|lt=Eternal youth}}. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}} categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni}} is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}} the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of {{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori}} travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}} to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]]

<!--unattributed in the printed book--><!--[[:ja:長谷川武次郎]], 弘文社--><!--国際日本文学研究集会会議録--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--成城大學經濟研究--><!--小林永濯 1843-1890--><!--彩流社--><!--4779113717, --><!--愛知淑徳大学論集一文学部・文学研究科篇--><!--比較文学--><!--|season=autumn-->

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]

{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "{{ill|Otherworld|en|Otherworld|WD=Q13383731|lt=Otherworld}}" though not necessarily seen as a place in the {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife}}, but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with {{ill|Eternal youth|en|Eternal youth|WD=Q836886|lt=Eternal youth}}. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}} categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni}} is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}} the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of {{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori}} travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}} to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}.{{efn|It is written as Horai ({{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid {{ill|162173 Ryugu|en|162173 Ryugu|WD=Q1385178|lt=162173 Ryugu}} has two {{ill|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|en|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|WD=Q1439394|lt=fossae}} named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]]

revision.comment: adding dummy category to make categories work
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]

{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "{{ill|Otherworld|en|Otherworld|WD=Q13383731|lt=Otherworld}}" though not necessarily seen as a place in the {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife}}, but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with {{ill|Eternal youth|en|Eternal youth|WD=Q836886|lt=Eternal youth}}. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga}} categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni}} is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi}} the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of {{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori}} travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}} to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}.{{efn|It is written as Horai ({{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai}}) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid {{ill|162173 Ryugu|en|162173 Ryugu|WD=Q1385178|lt=162173 Ryugu}} has two {{ill|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|en|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|WD=Q1439394|lt=fossae}} named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai|1=Mount Penglai}}.{{efn|It is written as Horai ({{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai|1=Mount Penglai}}) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid {{ill|162173 Ryugu|en|162173 Ryugu|WD=Q1385178|lt=162173 Ryugu|1=162173 Ryugu}} has two {{ill|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|en|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|WD=Q1439394|lt=fossae|1=Fossa (planetary nomenclature)}} named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]]

<!--unattributed in the printed book--><!--[[:ja:長谷川武次郎]], 弘文社--><!--国際日本文学研究集会会議録--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--成城大學經濟研究--><!--小林永濯 1843-1890--><!--彩流社--><!--4779113717, --><!--愛知淑徳大学論集一文学部・文学研究科篇--><!--比較文学--><!--|season=autumn-->
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
[[ja:常世の国]]
[[ko:토코요국]]
[[simple:Tokoyo]]
[[zh:常世國]]


{{wikidata link|Q11060259}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]

{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "{{ill|Otherworld|en|Otherworld|WD=Q13383731|lt=Otherworld|1=Otherworld}}" though not necessarily seen as a place in the {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife|1=Afterlife}}, but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with {{ill|Eternal youth|en|Eternal youth|WD=Q836886|lt=Eternal youth|1=Eternal youth}}. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}} is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of {{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori|1=Tajimamori}} travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]]

<!--unattributed in the printed book--><!--[[:ja:長谷川武次郎]], 弘文社--><!--国際日本文学研究集会会議録--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--苫小牧駒澤大学紀要--><!--成城大學經濟研究--><!--小林永濯 1843-1890--><!--彩流社--><!--4779113717, --><!--愛知淑徳大学論集一文学部・文学研究科篇--><!--比較文学--><!--|season=autumn-->

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]

{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "{{ill|Otherworld|en|Otherworld|WD=Q13383731|lt=Otherworld|1=Otherworld}}" though not necessarily seen as a place in the {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife|1=Afterlife}}, but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with {{ill|Eternal youth|en|Eternal youth|WD=Q836886|lt=Eternal youth|1=Eternal youth}}. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}} is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of {{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori|1=Tajimamori}} travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai|1=Mount Penglai}}.{{efn|It is written as Horai ({{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai|1=Mount Penglai}}) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid {{ill|162173 Ryugu|en|162173 Ryugu|WD=Q1385178|lt=162173 Ryugu|1=162173 Ryugu}} has two {{ill|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|en|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|WD=Q1439394|lt=fossae|1=Fossa (planetary nomenclature)}} named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]

{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "{{ill|Otherworld|en|Otherworld|WD=Q13383731|lt=Otherworld|1=Otherworld}}" though not necessarily seen as a place in the {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife|1=Afterlife}}, but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with {{ill|Eternal youth|en|Eternal youth|WD=Q836886|lt=Eternal youth|1=Eternal youth}}. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}} is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of {{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori|1=Tajimamori}} travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai|1=Mount Penglai}}.{{efn|It is written as Horai ({{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai|1=Mount Penglai}}) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid {{ill|162173 Ryugu|en|162173 Ryugu|WD=Q1385178|lt=162173 Ryugu|1=162173 Ryugu}} has two {{ill|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|en|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|WD=Q1439394|lt=fossae|1=Fossa (planetary nomenclature)}} named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

:

It is an "{{ill|Otherworld|en|Otherworld|WD=Q13383731|lt=Otherworld|1=Otherworld}}" though not necessarily seen as a place in the {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife|1=Afterlife}}, but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with {{ill|Eternal youth|en|Eternal youth|WD=Q836886|lt=Eternal youth|1=Eternal youth}}. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}} is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of {{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori|1=Tajimamori}} travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai|1=Mount Penglai}}.{{efn|It is written as Horai ({{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai|1=Mount Penglai}}) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid {{ill|162173 Ryugu|en|162173 Ryugu|WD=Q1385178|lt=162173 Ryugu|1=162173 Ryugu}} has two {{ill|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|en|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|WD=Q1439394|lt=fossae|1=Fossa (planetary nomenclature)}} named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tokoyo no kuni]][[ja:常世の国]][[ko:토코요국]][[simple:Tokoyo]][[zh:常世國]]

{{Short description|Location in Shinto mythology}}

:

{{nihongo|'''Tokoyo'''|常世|}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8740 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> also known as {{nihongo|2=隠世、幽世|3=Kakuriyo}}, or ''Taikaikan''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-02-19 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |access-date=2023-03-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180219090216/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_eos&data_id=23640 |archive-date=2018-02-19 }}</ref> is realm in [[Shinto]]. It is an "{{ill|Otherworld|en|Otherworld|WD=Q13383731|lt=Otherworld|1=Otherworld}}" though not necessarily seen as a place in the {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife|1=Afterlife}}, but rather as a mythical realm with many interpretations. It is believed to be a place where various [[kami]] and spirits of ancestors live with {{ill|Eternal youth|en|Eternal youth|WD=Q836886|lt=Eternal youth|1=Eternal youth}}. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} categorized three types of Tokoyo: a world of perpetual darkness, a world where people never grew old, and a world across the sea.<ref name=":4" />

{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}} is sometimes considered to be identical to Tokoyo no Kuni, or alternatively {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} the underworld.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=根の国 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/根の国-595461 |access-date=2018-02-18 |website={{lang|ja|日本大百科全書}} at kotobank.jp}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Bonnefoy |first1=Yves |title=Asian Mythologies |last2=Doniger |first2=Wendy |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1991 |isbn=0-226-06456-5 |pages=270–271}}</ref> There is no clear consensus on the relationship between these three realms.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

Various stories related to Tokoyo can be found in classics such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], including the tale of {{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori|1=Tajimamori}} travelling to Tokoyo in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} to help him become immortal. In the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] a fanatical cult dedicated to the god of Tokoyo was said to have emerged.<ref name=":4" />

== Myth of Okuninushi ==
{{Main|Ōkuninushi}}
In the [[Kojiki]], Ōkuninushi used to rule the world, but he relinquished control during the [[Kuni-yuzuri]] to transfer control to the [[Amatsukami]]. He made a request that [[Izumo-taisha|a magnificent palace]] – rooted in the earth and reaching up to heaven – be built in his honor, and then withdrew himself into the "less-than-one-hundred eighty-road-bendings" (百不足八十坰手 ''momotarazu yasokumade'', i.e. the unseen world of the spirit) and disappeared from the physical realm.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |pages=129–136}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref>

The son of [[Amaterasu]] [[Ame no Hohi]] was sent to run [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] for Susanoo when the transfer of land occurred as part of the agreement,<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=32 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref> and his descendants became the [[Izumo clan]] who run the shrine today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=2016 |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |doi=10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":22">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

== Identification with Mount Penglai ==
{{Main|Mount Penglai}}
In the story of [[Urashima Tarō]] Tokoyo is identified with {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai|1=Mount Penglai}}.{{efn|It is written as Horai ({{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Mount Penglai|1=Mount Penglai}}) in the straight Chinese text, but it is also annotated to indicate its should be read as Tokoyo-no-kuni.}}{{sfnp|McKeon|1996|pp=34, 65}}<ref name="poulton">{{citation |last=Poulton |first=M. Cody |title=Spirits of Another Sort: The Plays of Izumi Kyōka |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3TlkAAAAMAAJ |page=88 |year=2001 |publisher=Center for Japanese Studies, the University of Michigan |isbn=9780939512010}}</ref>

The Asteroid {{ill|162173 Ryugu|en|162173 Ryugu|WD=Q1385178|lt=162173 Ryugu|1=162173 Ryugu}} has two {{ill|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|en|Fossa (planetary nomenclature)|WD=Q1439394|lt=fossae|1=Fossa (planetary nomenclature)}} named after each one.<ref>https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2020/07/aa37096-19.pdf</ref>
